name.concept: Sound level crossing a threshold toggles the light state.

learn.concept: A clap can act like a switch. When the sound level rises above a chosen threshold, the LEDs toggle on or off.
learn.whyItWorks: The microphone measures sound energy over a short time window instead of reacting to one sample. The code compares that sound level to a threshold. If the sound is strong enough and enough time has passed since the last clap, the system changes state. This cooldown helps stop one clap from being counted many times.
learn.keyInsight: Threshold systems ignore small signals and only react when input becomes strong enough.
learn.realWorld: This same idea is used in clap lights, noise alarms, voice activity detection, and many sensor systems that wait for an event before acting.

system.input: An I2S microphone listens for sound level changes, and a push button changes the sensitivity level.
system.process: The board measures sound volume, compares it to the selected threshold, and checks a cooldown timer before toggling the light state.
system.output: The LED strip turns on or off, and the buzzer gives a short confirmation tone.

howItWorks.steps:
The system listens to sound and measures how strong it is.
A button press changes between high, medium, and low sensitivity levels.
If the measured sound rises above the active threshold, the system treats it like a clap event.
A cooldown timer prevents one clap from triggering multiple times.
The LEDs toggle on or off, and the buzzer gives a short feedback beep.

visual.led: All 7 LEDs glow warm orange when the light state is on and turn fully off when the state is off. During sensitivity selection, 1 to 3 green LEDs briefly indicate the chosen sensitivity level.
visual.buzzer: The buzzer gives a short high beep when the lights turn on, a lower beep when they turn off, and short beeps during sensitivity selection.

data.description: The serial output prints the strongest recent sound level, the active threshold, and whether the lights are on or off so students can see when a trigger happens.

steps: Connect the ESP32 board with a USB data cable.
Flash the project to the board.
Wait briefly for the microphone warm-up period.
Clap near the microphone to trigger the switch.
Press the button to cycle through sensitivity levels.
Watch the LED state, buzzer feedback, and serial data.
run.expected: A strong enough clap toggles the LEDs on or off, and changing the sensitivity changes how easily the clap is detected.

experiments.title: Compare sensitivity levels
experiments.baseline: Start in the medium sensitivity mode.
experiments.variable: Switch between high, medium, and low sensitivity and clap from the same distance each time.
experiments.observation: Higher sensitivity reacts more easily, while lower sensitivity needs a stronger clap.

experiments.title: Test clap distance
experiments.baseline: Choose one sensitivity mode and stand near the microphone.
experiments.variable: Move farther away and clap again from different distances.
experiments.observation: The trigger becomes less reliable as the clap gets weaker with distance.

experiments.title: Observe threshold behavior in noise
experiments.baseline: Run the system in a quiet room.
experiments.variable: Add background noise like talking or tapping and compare it with a real clap.
experiments.observation: Small sounds may stay below threshold, while a sharp clap crosses it and toggles the lights.

code.pseudocode: Read microphone level -> Compare with selected threshold -> If sound is strong enough and cooldown has passed, toggle light state -> Update LEDs, buzzer, and serial data
